1: I praise you, O LORD; though you were angry with me, your anger has turned away and you have comforted me. The YHWH-name is flagged — this is a personal address to the covenant God by name. The turning away of divine anger (shub) is the same verb for repentance — as Israel turns, God's anger turns away; as God's anger turns away, Israel can turn in praise. The MKT's though you were angry preserves the contrast that makes the praise meaningful: this is not cheap joy but joy after genuine judgment.
2: The LORD, the LORD himself, is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation. YHWH is flagged twice — the Hebrew reads Yah YHWH oz v'zimrat: the divine name appears in its short form Yah embedded in salvation (yeshuah) — this is the verbal root of the name Jesus (Yeshua). The MKT's rendering he has become my salvation preserves the verbal form. The verse is an almost verbatim quotation of the Song of Moses (Exod 15:2), framing the restoration as a new Exodus song.
3: With joy you will draw water from the springs of salvation. The springs of salvation (ma'ayanei hayeshuah) — salvation as water drawn from springs of divine life. The MKT preserves the Hebrew yeshuah (salvation) rather than substituting a synonym; the root of the name Jesus appears again. Jesus will develop this image: I am the living water (John 4:14; 7:38-39). Rev 7:17 closes the loop: the Lamb will lead them to springs of living water.
4: Give thanks to the LORD; call on his name; make his deeds known among the peoples. YHWH is flagged — the covenant name as the content of proclamation to the nations. Yada (know) is flagged — here make known (causative): Israel's calling is to publicize the divine name and deeds among the peoples. This missionary impulse — make God's deeds known to the nations — anticipates the Gentile mission of Acts (Acts 2:11; 13:47 citing Isa 49:6).
5: Sing praises to the LORD, for he has done glorious things; make this known throughout all the earth. Yada (know/make known) is flagged — again the causative: the knowledge of the LORD is to be caused to be known throughout the earth. The glorious things (ge'ut) are the specific acts of salvation — the new Exodus the previous chapter described. The MKT's glorious things is accurate; some versions use excellent things, but glorious better captures the weight of the Hebrew.
6: Cry aloud and shout for joy, you who dwell in Zion, for great is the Holy One of Israel in your midst. The chapter ends with the divine title Qadosh Yisrael (Holy One of Israel) — Isaiah's distinctive name for God, used 25 times in Isaiah, only 6 times in the rest of the OT. Great in your midst is the climax: the greatness of the Holy One is not an abstract attribute but a presence — he dwells in Zion. The NT equivalent is Immanuel: God with us.
